text.title: Mob Programming Antipatterns
text.summary: Failure modes that undermine mob cohesion, learning, or output quality.

antipattern.description: Tourist mob: most participants not engaged, scrolling phones, not following or contributing.
antipattern.reason: The mob's value is proportional to active participation. Disengaged observers get no knowledge transfer and drag the mob's energy down. Use round-robin navigation and active facilitation to prevent this.

antipattern.description: Expert dominance: one senior person monologues; juniors become typists without understanding.
antipattern.reason: The mob becomes a one-to-many knowledge transfer instead of collaborative construction. The expert's mental model stays with the expert. Enforce strong-style navigation and facilitator-managed turn-taking.

antipattern.description: No rotation: the driver stays at the keyboard until a task is "done".
antipattern.reason: Driver fatigue causes errors and reduces quality. Other team members disengage without the keyboard. Rotation is the practice's load-bearing constraint — without it, there is no mob.

antipattern.description: Mobbing everything: all tasks, all day, every day.
antipattern.reason: Mob programming is high-cost, high-value. Applying it to routine CRUD, dependency bumps, or simple refactors wastes the intensity. Reserve it for complex, knowledge-intensive, or high-stakes work.

The retrospective is where the mob learns to mob better. Without it, every session repeats the same friction: the same person dominates, breaks get skipped, the goal drifts. Problems persist indefinitely.

Two people is pairing, not mobbing. The mob's benefits (cross-discipline context, knowledge distribution, collective decision-making) require at least three participants. Start with the pair-programming methodology.
